sentences.text: Yes, this is that Polish--
Yeah, I left, yeah.
I came to-- I came to Krakau-- to Krakau.
And I know that in Prokocim, in that camp in Prokocim, I have my-- oh, I see, I forgot to tell.
I had my sister's husband was there.
So I walk in by myself into that camp.
Oh, when I came back to Poland, when I came back to Poland, they know that they-- he was in the same town from where I come.
They know that-- what was his name?
I forgot his name already-- is in Prokocim in a camp, that Polack know about him.
Polack know.
When-- that Polack, one more thing, that Polack, when I was walking in that town, Prokocim, going to work in the morning, so that Polack, came with that little girl.
He couldn't come in close when they took us to work, but that little girl came with a little second bread.
She gave me that, little Polack, Unbelievable.
She's alive now.
She's in Canada.
Her father wasn't-- because grown people, they didn't let stay close to the Jews when they took them to work.
But that little girl, she was smart enough.
She was playing there.
And when I was walking, she handed me bread.
It's unbelievable.
It's a lot of things.
It's a lot of things.
It's a lot of things what to tell.
And some of the Polish people was-- was good too, because in the places where we was working, when we came in the morning to work, it was laying bread on the ground.
They used to go at night and throw away bread, that when we come to work-- yes, some of them, they was nice, Polish people, yeah.
There was that place where-- that place where we was to work in Prokocim, the name was Rampa, Rampa.
They used to make the-- I don't know what they make.
They used to take-- the Russian tracks are wider than all in Europe or in the United States.
So--
The railroads, so they use to build-- because in this time, they-- I don't know why they built it.
They didn't needed nothing.
They just want to-- people to-- they take time to work to dick around, things like this.
But it was some German apprentice that-- yeah, when I was working on the late-- where was this?
In-- I forgot.
I forgot at that place.
Saint Valentin, that that foreman what was I was working in the late, so he used to come in the morning and tell me [NON-ENGLISH].
The Americans are already over here.
The Americans are over there-- over here, so watch out.
Watch out.
Don't do something wrong.
Because when they do something wrong, they start to hit you.
You finished, so long until they kill you.
So he used to tell me, very nice man.
I used to write to him.
Now I don't know what-- from Germany, I wrote to him letters to Leo Humpel Steimarc, yeah, very nice man.
Some of them, they was human.
But they used to kill, unbelievable.
A day in the camp?
A day in the camp.
A day in the camp, when you-- a day in the camp was like this. 6 or 7 o'clock in the morning, you go.
When you wake up, you go for coffee, black coffee.
And it was no-- it was no water, no nothing to wash yourself.
I didn't drink coffee, so I took that coffee and I wash myself with the coffee.
Because I don't-- the coffee was no good for nothing anyway.
And they-- that's all.
You went to work.
I think, my work, they gave you some soup.
That's all.
And you when you came home, they gave you-- you went in for soup, soup, potato soup, something, water with potato peels.
They didn't have.
They didn't have.
They gave you a piece of bread.
They gave you a piece of bread.
Oh, you have to watch it.
You have the uniforms, what you went to work, such-- like the people in the jails, things like this.
I still--
I just want to tell you why this.
I still now-- I have a pillow.
I don't sleep on the pillow.
I have the pillow over here-- I tell you why-- under my arms.
When you left outside in the front that uniform, you wake up in the morning, they steal it.
You don't have it.
When you don't have a uniform to go to work, they kill you.
So I put together that uniform, with that shoes, with that everything, and I put it over here under my arms, and I was sleeping.
Till now, I sleep with the pillow over here.
I don't have the pillow under my head.
I have this over here.
Because I couldn't fell to sleep another way.
So that's the way I was sleeping, with my-- whatever I have there, I put together the shoes inside, and put it on me and I was sleeping.
So nobody stole it.
What did you sleep on?
What was that like?
Nothing, wood.
From wood, and on wood, you were sleeping.
And this you had under your head, your uniform.
That's what you were sleeping here.
And there was-- they-- it's unbelievable.
It was at night-- at night, you have to go to the toilet or something else, you have to go in snow without shoes, without nothing to toilet at night.
And we used to laugh.
You're going be liberated, you go out, and the free wall to the chimney.
We used to laugh.
You want to go out?
You go out through the chimney, because they burn, the steam goes out through the chimney.
That's what they used to-- they used to-- terrible.

You could, for instance, when they transfer us from-- yeah, from the-- from where-- when they transfer us from Poland to Mauthausen, it was 200 people in a car, the same way like over here they transfer animals, horses, with the-- in the freight cars, they transfer us.
So we went--
We went-- they transfer us.
It took about four or five days.
It was so hot, not a little bit air, not water, no, nothing.
So from 200 people, about 15 of us came out alive.
The other one, they fainted out inside.
They're dead.
In that camp, yes.
I was--
I was young and healthy.
And I never-- I never gave up.
The worst thing was when you gave up.
You let yourself go.
I'm not going to live anywhere, you goodbye.
I always have the courage to tell-- to tell something-- I wouldn't tell this to a German, but to tell that Jewish fellow who kicked me.
I'm going to live through that hell.
I'm going to live through-- or when they told me to run around without shoes on the snow because I didn't come in into the camp straight like a soldier.
So I run around that camp.
And I say I'm going run the whole night, and I live through that hell.
I was 100% sure that I'm going to live through that hell.
And it was from-- it was almost from-- almost from-- in the camp, in the concentration camps, I was only from 1942 to 1945.
I have the papers over here.
But with the other labor camps, it's light from 1940, it's five years, from 1940 to 1945.
And in that cattle car that you were talking about where you were-- can you describe what actually-- I mean, how did people go to the bathroom?
The people, they didn't-- that was the worst thing.
You couldn't go to the bathroom, you couldn't go nothing.
That's why a lot of them, they fainted because the young people-- I was young in this time-- healthy people.
They could take this.
And it was older people, they couldn't take this, so they die.
They die.
When they opened that cattle car in Mauth-- no, we went-- that camp, that transport stopped in Auschwitz.
How do I know that at Auschwitz?
Because one conductor outside from the Germans asked the other one, what is this?
He tell them this is a [GERMAN].
So I heard this.
I thought, oh, it's good.
We're not going to be in Auschwitz.
We're going to go to Mauthausen.
Mauthausen wasn't worse than Auschwitz.
Mauthausen.
So it was lot of thing.
We used to-- we used to sleep outside, the few weeks what I was in Mauthausen, outside, because they didn't have nothing, where to sleep.
Just lay down like a piece of wood.
That's all.
Dirty.
And you didn't think about nothing.
You live like I'm completely like an animal.
I didn't think nothing about the family, about nothing.
The hunger, the hunger is terrible.
I just think my mind was occupied how to get something to eat.
And it was no way to get it.
I used to pick up coals, what they used to run the steam machine.
I used to pick up coals from the ground and chew it.
The war was finished, I used to go out with blood because from the constipation from that, everything.
You couldn't do nothing.
Yeah, typhus I got in the camp, in Prokocim.
They took me.
When it was still-- and Krakau was still a Jewish ghetto when they took me.
It was a hospital and ghetto too.
[NON-ENGLISH].
Yeah, I still remember.
Still remember.
The hospital?
Not long, maybe two weeks.
And they liquidated the ghetto.
I still wasn't 100% perfect.
They liquidated.
There was the ghetto liquidation.
You got those trombones.
You could hear the noises outside crying.
It was terrible.
In this time, a Jewish policeman by the name of Puntil, he knows me very good from that labor camp I was working in.
And he took me out from over there.
And he took me, he came.
It was-- no, it wasn't a hospital.
I don't what it was, just like a room.
And he took me out with a-- he got a-- in Europe, they didn't have taxis by the train or something, horses with that carriages.
It was a Polack horse and carriage, and he took me back to that camp.
That camp, in that camp, the camp fuhrer, he was a very nice man, a gentleman, Fischer, by the name of Fischer.
And I was working.
And while I was working, there's a blacksmith there.
And he used to-- I didn't go into the camp to sleep.
I slept on that-- on that-- where was I was working.
And I-- when I came to me very fast, and then they transfer us to another camp.
But that-- certain-- some of them-- oh, in that camp, in that place where I was working, he got in his house, that Fischer, he got like a machine shop and he had carpenters working.
That carpenters run away from him.
One day, they're not here.
So he says when he find out that they run away, I remember like today, he says in German, [GERMAN].
God help them.
[GERMAN].
God help them when they run away.
That was a very nice man, a German.
Some of them, they was-- yeah, it's-- it's coming to you like a dream, back everything.
It was-- was not so easy.
It was not so easy.
But I cool off mine everything when I came back to Germany after the war.
I was going around just looking for trouble.
How you called it, [NON-ENGLISH].
Advantage?
Not advantage.
[GERMAN] they sell it in Germany.
[NON-ENGLISH], they sell it in Jewish, [NON-ENGLISH].
I want to take a--
Revenge.
Revenge, yeah, that's what it was.
And I want to take revenge.
I did everything is possible.
I did a lot of things.
For instance-- for instance, they used to travel-- the Germans, during the war, they used to travel from one place to the other.
So on the train station, when they wait at night, hundreds, they're waiting from one train after the other.
I used to come-- there was a few boys like me that was healthy and we took revenge.
I used to knock the hell out of them.
They used to call [GERMAN].
Jewish people are killing us.
Say you kill us too.
Yeah, I take revenge a little bit for them.
I used to tell them, this is for my mother, this is for my father.
This is from my sister and brother.
I used to give them the business.
Where is-- we wasn't born from a stone in Polish.
Where are our property?
They used to take away-- they took away all the properties and gave away to the Polacks.
We wasn't born just like this.
We have a house.
We have everything.
So I used to do-- You took out your anger?
Yeah, it was something.
I had a KL.
I am not exactly-- I think I have this by Schindler, by Schindler.
But because, I think, in another place-- in another place, I wouldn't be able to go and suck it out.
So I got it by Schindler.
They use to give KL, Koncentration Lager.
But it was big, that when you run away, they see right away you have a KL on the hand.
I have it still.
You could see it over here.
You could see it over here.
KL, but it's very little.
I suck it--
Yeah, I suck it out.
My wife has a number from Auschwitz.
I have a number, yeah. 86,500-- 86,511.
Because I didn't have a name like Nathan or Fleischer.
This was my name, 86,000-- I have in it on that release, on that liberation card from the Germans, I have it.
Because I didn't have a name there.
It was 86,511 was my name.
I think I had this-- this not in the labor camps still, by Schindler, I think, I had it.
By Schindler, I have this.
I think so.
It's like I remember I went into the restrooms there, and I had it by Schindler.
Schindler was-- Schindler, it was a lot of young girls by Schindler from Krakau.
He was very good looking man, very good looking, tall, handsome.
They all was in love with him.
So to take-- to have his attention, they used to cut up a little bit that finger.
So he saw blood, so he took them in the hand into the hospital.
They loved this.
Schindler, yeah, he used to.
Well, those people, what they was liberated with him, they went from Krakau-Plaszow, they went-- oh, Brnenec, in Czechoslovakia.
In Brnenec, it was the German police and the SS.
Before the Russian came, before the liberation came, he tell the police-- he tell the police the war is over.
You could go home, to the police, wherever you come.
So the police left.
They left the camp without police.
And the Jewish people was going away or whatever they want to do, before the Russians liberate them, Schindler tell this.
He tell to the police the war is over already.
You don't have to stay over here and watch them.
And they left.
This was Schindler.
Yeah.
Also good looking man, very nice.
He wasn't an angel, but-- I mean, he wasn't a-- how you call this?
He wasn't a saint, but he was an angel.
It shows you, you don't have to be a saint to be an angel.
He was a human being.
He knows the people are working for him.
Food, that's the only place what you didn't go around hungry.
Whenever you felt-- whenever you was hungry, you went to the kitchen.
The kitchen was open.
They gave you whatever-- whatever they had.
Their cook was a Jewish woman cooking there.
So it was all right.
Schindler, what was-- they used to play-- what a lot of young people from Krakau, they used to belong to the Jewish Soccer Team, the Maccabi in Krakau.
So they used to play soccer there in the camp on Sunday, when-- whenever we didn't go to work.
It was the Jewish-- by Schindler, the Jewish chief of the police was [NON-ENGLISH].
He had a girlfriend by the name of Bronca.
I remember this everything.
Was a policeman there from Krakau by the name of Mischa Gellar.
And I was with a few-- they was-- they was with Schindler all the way through the camp, Solomon Urba, he's on the picture there.
They show him on the film too.
I saw him on television once.
I spoke to him.
Just happened, he's very well off now, after the war.
He was working as a carpenter.
He was working as a carpenter.
And when the war was finished, I think so, he built himself a house.
And when the war was with Korea, a soldier came home, and he got the allowance from the government money or something, and he bought that house by him.
And he built another house.
He became a builder.
He make some money.
He's in Orange, New Jersey he lives.
I'm in contact with him.
And another-- and another very close friend of mine, he just died in Israel.
He-- when I was in Israel-- Adam Mandel, his name, he married.
He lived in Israel until about two months ago.
He died.
He was a very close, very close friend of mine.
And I got over here.
I call up that guy.
What-- we went to from one camp to the other.
When we came to the concentration camp in Krakau-Plaszow, he was a little boy, 11 years old.
In the door, while we was going to the camp, they used to take out all the children to kill.
And in it was a Polish-- I don't know.
He was something, a Polish doctor or something, by the name of Gross, I think, Dr.
Gross.
He used to take out the children from the line.
But he was small, short.
We know about this, so we put him on a hat, a high hat, and shoes from a woman with high heels.
And when we walk, he was in the middle, his uncle from the left side, I from the right side.
And we raise him like this, that he was the same height what our.
And it was there thousands of people.
You couldn't see how he walked.
And he was going in the air.
And he is, today-- he is, today, he lives in New York.
He's a professor from languages.
He's going to be in February in my house.
His uncled died.
If you would talk to him, he would tell you that if not me and his uncle, he wouldn't be alive today.
Then he was a very good looking kid, and he was working by the SS, by the dogs.
They have dogs.
So they gave the dogs better food than us.
So he used to bring that dog-- that food what they gave to the dogs, he used to bring to us.
And the dogs die out of hunger.
He was a very smart kid.
He's a professor.
He came-- he was young.
When he came over here, he was in the army, and then he went to school.
And he became a professor.
French and Russian he used to teach.
Now, I think, he's retired because he got some relatives there.
They died, and he-- he pick up his-- he could live without working.
He's not working now.
But he goes to Israel.
He goes-- he comes to me for-- it was for two weeks, he comes to me, stay with me.
Just happened-- just happened that I went to an eye doctor, just recently, a month ago.
The eye doctor's name is Getz, the same name what my friend's name.
So I told him I have a very close friend by the name of Ira Getz.
So he told me from where is he?
So I told him from this and this town in Poland.
So he told me, it's possible I am related to him.
I'm a town further, 11 kilometer from Gorlitz, he is.
So I gave him his name, and I call him up.
And they got in touch.
And they are some far away relatives.
But that guy, that doctor, I wouldn't recognize.
He was-- because the camp, I told him what camps what I was, so he know everything about this.
So I didn't know that he was in the camps too.
But when I talk to that-- my friend in New York, he told me that he was in the same camps what I was.
And he became a doctor when he came after the war, and he has an office, eye doctor.
So right across the street where I live, Dr.
Getz, it's a small world.
Sure, I tell you right away a miracle from Prokocim.
I told you, I think, already when they-- yeah, I told you already this.
This, I told you.
It's miracles.
It's miracles.
Oh, when I was-- when I was-- I wasn't sleeping in the camp.
I was taking care of that night-- at night, a water pump at night.
So I was sleeping on that [GERMAN], they call it, such a little from wood-- they have an oven there.
So they left once a girl there because they was afraid that they're going to kill-- they're going to take out from the camp the girls, the young kids.
And a Polish woman by the name of Huka Shiska is going to pick her up from my place at night.
So it was like this that Huka Shiska picked up that girl, but it was snow on the ground.
And I was afraid the SS going to bring the Jewish people to work in the morning.
They're going to follow the footsteps, and they could follow all the way to that Polish woman.
So before I went to sleep, I went outside and I says, God, don't take care of me, but take care of that little girl.
Make a little bit snow.
So help me God.
And I fell to sleep two hours later.
And when I go out, I couldn't open the door, full of snow.
It's unbelievable.
But you see, since that time, I am hesitating, something exist in the world.
And that's all.
You see such-- such a little things, but they would come in the morning, they see the steps.
They could follow all the way to that Polish woman and-- that little girl is not today little.
She is married.
She is in Israel.
A lot of things.
[SOB]
Miracles, yeahs, miracles happen.
Miracles happen every minute.
Child is born, it's a miracle.
The day of liberation was May the 6th, 1945, 2 o'clock in the afternoon.
Right after-- right after the liberation, you were so weak, you couldn't do a lot.
You couldn't walk.
For instance, we was sleeping-- we was sleeping in the same place where we was working, where we was during the Germans.
So we was sleeping.
They gave us more to eat already.
But I was so weak that I had, for instance, I want to go out at night.
When I want to go at night, so it was a little step.
I couldn't walk to that step.
I went on my knees.
I was so weak.
